SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper clearly states its main claims and provides empirical evidence: it defines sentence- and word-level KL objectives (Eq. 9–11) and reports BLEU improvements over pivot and standard NMT baselines (Sections 3 and 4). The mathematical setup (KL divergences, teacher-student objective) is sensible, but there are heuristic approximations for JSENT and JWORD (Section 4.2) and no formal proof that the assumptions imply good end-to-end translation. Experimental methodology is reasonable, but the paper does not report statistical significance tests or full details of approximation errors, leaving minor concerns about robustness of some claims.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The idea of using a pivot-to-target 'teacher' to train a source-to-target 'student' in a zero-resource setting is a novel application of knowledge distillation ideas to pivot-based zero-resource NMT (Section 3, related work cites Kim & Rush 2016). The problem (zero-resource NMT) is well-studied, but the paper differentiates itself by introducing sentence- and word-level teaching assumptions and algorithms (Sections 3.1 and 3.3). The contribution is a non-trivial extension of existing distillation and pivot methods rather than a purely incremental reuse of prior techniques.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is organized into assumptions, methods, and experiments (Sections 3–4) and key equations (9–11) are presented; the main contributions are described early (intro + Section 3). However, the provided text contains many duplicated and repeated passages and some presentation noise, which reduces readability and makes the narrative and exposition less polished. Notation for objectives and KL is standard and mostly consistent, but the redundancy and editorial issues impede a fully clear reading.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The experiments evaluate multiple language pairs and corpora (Europarl, WMT) and compare sentence-level and word-level variants, sampling vs mode approximations, and a low-resource source-to-pivot setting (Sections 4.2–4.3, Tables 1, 4, 5, 7 referenced). The paper also compares to several prior methods (pivot, multilingual, transfer). Missing are detailed ablation/sensitivity analyses (e.g., hyperparameters, variance across runs) and explicit failure-case discussion, and some reproducibility details are not provided in the excerpt.

IMPACT.justification: Zero-resource NMT is an important practical problem and the proposed teacher-student approach yields meaningful BLEU gains over pivot-based baselines and can even match standard NMT in some settings (Section 4.3.4), suggesting practical value. The approach is likely to inform future work on distillation and multilingual transfer, though it is not obviously transformative since it builds on existing distillation and pivot ideas.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: The topic (zero-resource neural machine translation) and methods (teacher-student distillation, empirical BLEU evaluation on standard corpora) are squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML venues. The evaluation protocols and baselines used (e.g., Cheng et al. 2016, Firat et al., Zoph et al.) match community standards (Sections 4 and Related Work).

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares against appropriate and recent baselines including pivot-based methods, multilingual approaches, and transfer learning (Sections 4.3, Related Work; Tables compare to Cheng et al., Firat et al., Zoph et al.). The authors note dataset-size differences where relevant (Firat uses larger training sets) and use the same data for direct comparisons in some experiments, but the excerpt lacks detailed fairness controls (e.g., same model sizes/hyperparameters across all baselines) and statistical testing.
